Concluding Unscientific Postscript to Philosophical Fragments
Journal of the Royal Asiatic Society
List of people considered a founder in a humanities field
Seven Sages of the Bamboo Grove
Way of the Five Pecks of Rice

Philosophy of thermal and statistical physics
The Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences
The Singular Universe and the Reality of Time

nodes.description: The philosophical position that reality exists independently of human perception and thought.
The view that reality consists of multiple distinct fundamental substances or kinds.
Entities that are non-physical, non-mental, and exist outside space and time, such as numbers and universals.
The concept that reality has a foundational level of entities or facts that explain all other levels.
The idea that objects and facts exist regardless of being perceived or conceptualized by any mind.
The philosophic conception of the world as the sum total of all entities, the whole of reality or everything that exists.
The idea that there are many distinct worlds, ranging from modal possible worlds to cosmological multiverses.
The world considered as the ultimate horizon or background of all experience, as developed in phenomenology.
The epistemological contrast between the internal mental realm and the external physical world, central to philosophy of mind.
The concept of the world as created by God, identical to God, or interdependent with the divine, as explored in philosophy of religion.
A comprehensive, often culturally shared, philosophical framework that defines one's understanding of the world and humanity's place within it.
The philosophical debate over whether existence is a first-order property of individuals or a second-order property of concepts.
The distinction between the existence of individual entities and the existence of concepts, universals, or abstract objects.
The ontological distinction between entities that exist in space and time and entities that exist abstractly, such as numbers or propositions.
The logical analysis of existence as expressed by the existential quantifier in predicate logic.
The primary category of being, that which exists independently and underlies all other categories.
The category of being that indicates where something is located in space.
The category of being that describes the arrangement or posture of a substance, such as 'sitting' or 'standing'.
The category of being that refers to a condition or habitus of a substance, such as being armed or clothed.
The category of being that denotes a passive experience or undergoing, where a substance is acted upon.
The view that only the actual world is real; possible worlds are abstract representations or fictions.
Conditional statements about what would be the case if something were different, central to modal reasoning.
The view that causation is nothing more than constant conjunction of events, as per David Hume's analysis.
The theory that causal relations are grounded in counterfactual dependencies: if the cause had not occurred, the effect would not have occurred.
The view that causation is a physical process involving the transmission of a conserved quantity or mark.
The idea that causation is understood in terms of possible interventions: A causes B if manipulating A changes B.
The classification of causes into necessary (effect cannot occur without it), sufficient (it alone produces the effect), and contributing factors.
The ontological categories of entities that can stand in causal relations, such as events, facts, states, or processes.
The view that mind and body are two fundamentally different substances that can exist independently.
The thesis that the laws of nature determine everything that happens.
The doctrine that all events are necessary, leaving no possibility of alternative outcomes.
The idea that all events have been predetermined, often by a prior cause or destiny.
The belief that a supreme being predetermines everything that occurs.
The view that determinism holds at the macroscopic level but not necessarily at the quantum level.
The position that free will exists and requires that determinism is false, often invoking indeterministic causal processes such as agent causation or non‑deterministic choice.
Immediate apprehension of truth through reason alone, as in Descartes' cogito or Spinoza's intuitive knowledge.
The capacity for immediate sensory representation, including pure forms of intuition (space and time) that structure experience.
The direct perception of ethical truths without inference, central to ethical intuitionism.
The mental construction of mathematical objects as the foundation of mathematical knowledge, defended by Brouwer's intuitionism.
Immediate, non-dual knowledge of reality as in Buddhist prajna or Hindu satori, often contrasted with discursive reasoning.
The metaphysical doctrine that universals (properties, relations, types) have objective, mind-independent existence, grounding the similarity of distinct particulars.
The view that universals are mere names or linguistic conventions with no extra-mental reality; only particular objects exist fundamentally.
The position that universals exist only as mental concepts or abstractions in the mind, not as independently real entities.
The theory that non-material abstract Forms are the most fundamental reality, from which the material world derives.
The metaphysical view that properties exist independently of the objects that instantiate them.
The view that properties do not have mind-independent existence, including nominalism and conceptualism.
The view that properties are categorical, meaning they are what they are independently of any dispositions or causal powers.
The view that properties are essentially dispositional, defined by their potential manifestations and causal roles.
A property that an object possesses solely in virtue of itself, independent of external relations.
A property that an object possesses only in relation to other things or its environment.
A property that an object must have to be the very kind of thing it is; without it the object would not be itself.
A property that an object could lose or gain without altering its identity.
A specific, fully determinate property that falls under a more general determinable, such as 'scarlet' under 'red'.
The phenomenon where time passes at different rates for observers in relative motion or different gravitational fields.
The concept that time repeats in cycles, often found in ancient and Eastern philosophies.
A metaphysical distinction where an essential property is necessary for a thing to be what it is, while an accidental property can change without altering the thing's identity.

Locke's distinction: real essence is the unknown internal constitution causing observable qualities; nominal essence is the abstract idea of the kind formed by the mind from shared observable qualities.
Aristotle's essence as the substantial form that makes a substance what it is versus Husserl's phenomenological essence as an ideal structure grasped by consciousness through eidetic reduction.
nodes.id: Essence as Form vs Essence as Intentional Object

nodes.description: The existentialist thesis, notably defended by Sartre, that for human beings there is no predetermined essence; one first exists, then defines oneself through actions.
Kit Fine's view that essence is not reducible to modality (necessity), but concerns what it is to be a thing, grounding metaphysical necessity in the nature of objects.
The most fundamental concept in ontology, referring to all of reality and every entity within it.
Distinction between unique, non-repeatable entities (particulars) and general, repeatable entities (universals).
Distinction between objects existing in space and time (concrete) and those existing outside space and time (abstract).
Dispute over whether universals, mathematical objects, or unobservable entities exist objectively or only in the mind or language.
A relation that depends solely on the intrinsic (monadic) properties of the entities it relates.
A relation that is not determined solely by the intrinsic properties of its relata, thus adding new information beyond them.
The metaphysical stance that relations are mere mental abstractions and do not exist in external reality.
The view that relations can be fully explained in terms of monadic properties of the relata, without positing an additional ontological category.
The position that relations have mind-independent existence, being as real as the entities they connect.
A functor is a mapping between categories that preserves the composition and identity structure, allowing translation between different categorical contexts.
A natural transformation is a family of morphisms between the images of two functors, providing a way to relate functors and capturing the idea of a systematic transformation.
An adjunction is a pair of functors between categories that satisfy a natural bijection of hom-sets, representing a fundamental notion of duality and universal property.
Limits and colimits are universal constructions in a category that generalize products, equalizers, and pullbacks (limits) and coproducts, coequalizers, and pushouts (colimits).
A universal property characterizes an object by its unique relation to other objects via a universal arrow, often used to define categorical constructions uniquely up to isomorphism.
A philosophical view that structures (categorical relationships) are primary over objects, influencing debates on mathematical ontology and structural realism in philosophy of science.
The doctrine that all mental phenomena are directed toward an object, whether existent or nonexistent.
The ontological theory that there is an object for every mental state, including nonexistent objects that have being without existence.
A logical strategy distinguishing between exemplifying and encoding properties to account for nonexistent objects like the round square.
Bertrand Russell's argument that Meinong's theory leads to contradictions, particularly regarding the law of non-contradiction.
The separation of reality into an intelligible realm of perfect, eternal Forms and a sensible realm of imperfect, changing appearances.
The theory that physical objects have their properties by participating in or copying the corresponding Form.
The highest Form in Plato's ontology, the ultimate source of reality and intelligibility, analogous to the sun in the Allegory of the Cave.
A logical critique showing that the theory of Forms leads to an infinite regress if Forms are considered to have the properties they represent.
An extended metaphor for the journey from ignorance to philosophical enlightenment, where prisoners mistake shadows for reality and the sun represents the Form of the Good.
Inherence is the relation between a substance and its properties, whereby the properties are borne by the substance yet distinct from it.
The concept of continuous quantity or size, often contrasted with number, and related to measurement and geometry.
The assignment of numbers to objects or properties to represent quantity, involving units and scales.
The distinction between quantities that are countable (discrete) and those that are measurable (continuous), a fundamental ontological dichotomy.
The categories of modality in Kant's system: possibility, existence, necessity.
The philosophical view that numbers exist as abstract, non-physical objects independent of human thought.
The view that numbers are merely formal symbols manipulated according to rules with no inherent meaning.
The view that numbers are mental constructions and mathematical truth is subjective, based on constructive proof.
The philosophical treatment of zero as a number representing nothingness and its foundational role in arithmetic.
The philosophical exploration of infinite numbers, the nature of the infinite, and its paradoxes.
The metaphysical inquiry into the necessary and sufficient conditions under which a collection of objects composes a further object.
The thesis that any arbitrary collection of objects always composes a whole, regardless of spatial or causal relations.
The view that a composite object is numerically identical to its parts taken collectively, challenging the distinctness of whole and parts.
The fundamental relation of being a part of a whole, which forms the basis of mereological systems.
The notion that any collection of objects has a fusion or sum that is a whole composed of them.
The distinction between theories with fundamental atomic parts and theories where everything is infinitely divisible (gunk).
The principle that all matter consists of tiny, solid, indivisible, and indestructible particles called atoms, which differ only in shape, size, and motion.
The concept that atoms move through an otherwise empty void, which is necessary for their rearrangement and the possibility of change.
The view that all natural phenomena result solely from the mechanical interactions (collisions and combinations) of atoms in the void, without purpose or teleology.

The idea that complex observable wholes are not fundamental realities; only atoms and void truly exist, and properties of wholes are explained by the properties and arrangements of their atomic parts.
A perfect, eternal, and non-physical essence that is the true reality, of which physical objects are imperfect copies.
The realm of Forms, accessible only by intellectual reasoning, in contrast to the sensible realm of physical objects perceived by the senses.
The theory that learning is the recollection of innate knowledge of Forms from the soul's pre-existence.
Concepts are defined by necessary and sufficient conditions that determine membership.
Concepts are represented by a prototype of typical features and membership is graded by similarity.
Concepts are represented by stored memories of individual instances rather than a summary representation.
Concepts are embedded in domain-specific theories that explain phenomena.
Lexical concepts are primitive units without internal structure.
The metaphysical investigation into whether natural kinds have essences, how they relate to laws of nature and necessity, and what grounds their reality.
The epistemological study of how we can know and reliably classify natural kinds, including the role of inductive inference and scientific methodology.
The examination of how natural kinds function in scientific theories, taxonomy, explanation, and the realism–anti‑realism debate about theoretical entities.
nodes.id: Natural Kinds in Philosophy of Science

nodes.description: The analysis of how natural‑kind terms (e.g., 'water', 'tiger') refer rigidly and the contributions of Kripke and Putnam to theories of meaning and reference.
nodes.id: Natural Kinds in Philosophy of Language

nodes.description: The stance that we should believe in the existence and properties of unobservable entities posited by mature scientific theories.
The claim that scientific statements have truth-conditions that refer to a mind-independent reality, and that our best theories are approximately true.
The argument that the empirical success of science would be miraculous if our theories were not at least approximately true; hence, realism is the best explanation of scientific progress.
The historical argument that many past successful scientific theories have later been discarded as false, so present theories are likely false as well; used as a challenge to scientific realism.
A selective version of realism that commits to the existence of unobservable entities while remaining agnostic about the truth of the laws or theories that describe them.
A version of realism that holds that knowledge is limited to the structural relations of the world, while the nature of the underlying entities may be unknowable.
A fact that is constituted by collective intentionality or social agreement, such as the fact that a piece of paper is money, distinct from brute physical facts.
A category of entities, such as race or gender, that exists in virtue of social conventions and practices, akin to natural kinds but ontologically dependent on society.
A collection of individuals that forms a unified entity with its own properties, such as a nation or a committee, raising questions about identity and persistence over time.
The descriptive thesis that a given discourse is actually engaged in pretense or make-believe rather than asserting literal truths.
The prescriptive thesis that we should adopt a fictionalist attitude toward a discourse for pragmatic reasons.
The view that mathematical statements are useful fictions that do not aim at literal truth.
The view that moral statements are useful fictions rather than literal truths.
The formal use of possible worlds to interpret modal and intensional logic by evaluating truth across worlds.
The view that possible worlds exist as concrete, mind-independent entities, not merely as abstract representations or linguistic constructions.
The claim that possible worlds are of the same fundamental kind as the actual world, differing only in the particular facts that obtain.
The thesis that possible worlds cannot be reduced to more basic entities such as sets of propositions or linguistic descriptions; they are primitive.
The view that 'actual' is an indexical term, so that any world is actual from the perspective of its inhabitants.
The similarity-based relation that connects individuals across possible worlds in counterpart theory, replacing trans-world identity.
The claim that no individual exists in more than one possible world; each world has distinct individuals.
The denial that individuals have a primitive 'thisness' that can be tracked across worlds, making identity across worlds a matter of qualitative similarity.
The specific axioms for quantified modal logic developed by David Lewis that formalize the counterpart relation and worlds.
The analogy between modal counterparts and temporal parts, used to motivate counterpart theory by comparing persistence through time with existence across worlds.
The philosophical view that space and time are not absolute entities but merely relational properties of the positions and interactions of material objects.
The view that mathematical objects are defined solely by their relationships within a structure, having no intrinsic essence apart from that structure.
The metaphysical position that relations are primary and that objects and properties are derivative from relational networks.
The ordering of events as past, present, and future, involving tensed properties that change over time.
The ordering of events by the tenseless relations of earlier than and later than, involving permanent temporal relations.
The experiential or metaphysical notion that time flows or ‘passes’, with events coming into and out of existence, as contrasted with a static block universe.
The idea that a cause increases the probability of its effect relative to the baseline.
A statistical relation where a third variable renders the cause and effect conditionally independent, indicating a causal structure.
The principle that correlated events are typically due to a shared common cause.
A statistical phenomenon where aggregate data shows a trend opposite to that in the constituent subgroups, relevant to causal inference.
The use of directed acyclic graphs to represent and reason about causal relationships.
The concept of manipulating a variable to determine its causal effect, central to causal modeling.
The analysis of causation in specific token events as opposed to general type-level causation.
The philosophical position that perception yields direct, unmediated awareness of the external world.
The view that we perceive the external world indirectly, through internal mental representations or sense data.
The epistemological challenge that perception cannot provide certain or reliable knowledge of the external world.
The philosophical issue raised by perceptual illusions and hallucinations, questioning the veridicality and directness of perceptual experience.
The raw data from sensory organs, distinct from perception, often discussed in epistemology and philosophy of mind.
The experience of feeling or emotion, particularly as studied in phenomenology and affective science.
The philosophical view that emotions are essentially conscious feelings or subjective experiences.
In Ancient Greek philosophy, desire is categorized as a broad genus (orexis) including specific kinds such as epithumia (appetite), boulêsis (wish), and thumos (spiritedness).
Plato's view that individual desires must be postponed for a higher ideal, and that the soul's passionate horse must be guided by reason to discern correct objects of desire.
Aristotle's theory that desire is involved in animal motion and that reason, together with desire, determines the good object of desire, distinguishing appetite from volition.
In Buddhism, craving (tanha) is the most potent form of desire, identified as the cause of suffering, which is to be eliminated to attain Nirvana.
In psychoanalysis (Freud, Lacan, Deleuze and Guattari), desire is a central concept linked to unconscious drives, lack, and schizoanalysis.
The doctrine that exactly one concrete object token exists.
The doctrine that exactly one concrete object token is basic, and the whole is prior to its proper parts.
The doctrine that all concrete objects fall under one highest type, such as material, mental, or neutral.
The view that the fundamental constituents of reality are pure experiences, which are neutral between mental and physical aspects.
The view that the ultimate nature of the world is composed of neutral events or that the intrinsic properties of physical entities are protomental.
The view that actions are bodily movements caused by intentions in the right way, as proposed by Donald Davidson.
The view that actions involve a distinct mental act of volition (an act of will) that directly causes the bodily movement.
The view that intentions are not causes of actions but are constituents of them, emphasizing reasons as justifications rather than causes.
An action that an agent does without doing any other action, serving as the fundamental unit of action.
An action that occurs in the mind, such as deciding or imagining, as opposed to bodily movements.
The philosophical problem of how mental events can causally influence physical actions given the apparent causal closure of the physical domain.
The concept that probabilistic outcomes are governed by underlying deterministic processes, as in hidden variable theories.
The interpretation of quantum mechanics in which all possible outcomes occur in branching universes, often taken as a form of determinism.
A version of libertarianism that holds that free actions are caused by the agent as a substance, not by prior events, requiring indeterminism at the level of agent causation.
The status of a being that is capable of acting with reference to right and wrong, and thus can be held morally responsible.
The requirement that a moral agent must know or be reasonably aware of the moral significance of their actions to be held responsible.
The concept that groups, organizations, or communities can be held morally responsible for outcomes, not just individuals.
The principle that each thing is identical to itself, expressed as 'A is A'.
The distinction between being exactly similar (qualitative identity) and being the same object (numerical identity).
nodes.id: Distinction between qualitative and numerical identity

nodes.description: A thought experiment that questions whether an object that has all its components replaced remains the same object.
The philosophical analysis of statements asserting that two names refer to the same thing, e.g. 'Hesperus is Phosphorus'.
The view that personal identity consists in the continuity of the same living body over time.
The view that personal identity consists in the continuity of memory, consciousness, or psychological characteristics (originating with Locke).
The theory that the self is not a unified substance but a collection of transient perceptions and mental states (associated with Hume).
The denial of a permanent, substantial self; the self is considered an illusion or mere convention (found in Buddhist philosophy).
Philosophical implications of split-brain cases for the unity and continuity of personal identity.
The concept that being a person entails having outstanding moral worth, which grounds special rights and protections.
The view that personhood is defined by sophisticated cognitive capacities such as rationality, self-awareness, autonomy, or the ability to hold beliefs and desires.
The idea that personhood is not an inherent property but emerges from social relationships and interactions, often emphasizing communal or processual aspects.
The status of being a legal person (natural or juridical) under law, conferring rights, privileges, responsibilities, and legal liability.
The legal doctrine granting personhood to corporations, allowing them to have rights and be held liable as a single entity.
The philosophical and ethical question of when a human organism acquires personhood, often debated in contexts such as abortion and fetal rights.
The formal definition of truth for formal languages using satisfaction conditions, which avoids paradoxes.
The abstract, mind-independent content of a declarative sentence that is the primary bearer of truth or falsity, often considered the common meaning shared by different sentence-tokens.
A linguistic entity, either as an abstract type (pattern of words) or a concrete token (inscription or utterance), that can be evaluated as true or false in a given context.
A particular act of asserting a proposition, often tied to a specific speaker, time, and place, which can be assessed for truth or falsity.
A mental state of holding a proposition to be true, which is commonly considered a truth-bearer in epistemology and philosophy of mind.
A particular spoken or written act of producing a sentence, considered as a truth-bearer in contexts where context and performance matter.
A cognitive act or state of affirming or denying a proposition, often treated as the fundamental truth-bearer in certain logical and epistemological frameworks.
The metaphysical principle that truth depends on being; for any truth, there must be something in reality that makes it true.
The entity in the world (e.g., a fact, state of affairs, or object) that serves as the ontological ground for a truthbearer's truth.
The relation between a truthmaker and a truthbearer, often understood in terms of necessitation or grounding, whereby the existence of the former ensures the truth of the latter.
Truth is defined as coherence within a specified set of propositions, where coherence primarily means logical consistency.
Truth is defined as systematic coherence that involves more than logical consistency, requiring a holistic fit of concepts and content.
A method for clarifying the meaning of concepts by tracing their practical consequences.
The view that a belief is true if it proves useful or satisfactory in guiding action and inquiry.
The view that truth is the ideal outcome of indefinitely prolonged scientific inquiry.
nodes.id: Truth as the end of inquiry

nodes.description: The material adequacy condition that any viable theory of truth must entail, for every sentence 'P', a biconditional of the form 'P is true iff P'.
The distinction between the language being discussed (object language) and the language used for discussion (metalanguage), necessary to avoid semantic paradoxes like the liar.
A self-referential paradox exemplified by 'This sentence is false', which semantic theories of truth aim to resolve by restricting self-reference.
A deflationary theory holding that the truth predicate is redundant; asserting that a statement is true adds no content beyond the statement itself.
A deflationary theory that treats the truth predicate as a performative act rather than a descriptive property.
A deflationary theory that the truth predicate serves to disquote sentences, allowing expression of infinite conjunctions or generalizations.
A deflationary theory that truth is a prosentence-forming operator akin to anaphora in language.
A deflationary theory that truth is a minimal property defined by the equivalence schema 'p' is true iff p.
Knowledge that is justified independently of experience, often derived from reason or innate ideas.
Knowledge that is justified by empirical evidence or sensory experience.
The philosophical notion that experience consists of the stream of conscious events where a subject is presented with objects and qualities.
The concept of experience as the practical knowledge and familiarity gained through direct perceptual engagement with the world.
An epistemological critique that rejects the idea that there are non-conceptual sensory givens that can serve as a foundation for knowledge.
The thesis that when we introspect our experiences, we are only aware of the objects of experience, not the experience itself.
The recollection of specific past events from one's personal life, involving experiential reliving.
The conscious experience of emotions such as pleasure, pain, fear, and joy.
The experience of desire and the sense of being an agent who initiates actions.
The epistemological thesis that we have no access to a mind-independent reality, even if it exists, making knowledge of the external world impossible.
nodes.id: Epistemic Skepticism about the External World

nodes.description: A property of deductive arguments where the conclusion necessarily follows from the premises, making it impossible for the premises to be true and the conclusion false.
A property of deductive arguments that are both valid and have all true premises, ensuring the conclusion is true.
A logical schema that permits deriving a conclusion from a set of premises based solely on their logical form, such as modus ponens or modus tollens.
An error in the logical structure of a deductive argument that renders it invalid, often violating a rule of inference.
The philosophical view that causal relations can be analyzed in terms of counterfactual dependencies between events.
A psychological distinction between imagining how an outcome could have been better (upward) or worse (downward), with implications for emotion and motivation.
A perfect physical duplicate of a conscious human that lacks subjective experience or qualia entirely.
The philosophical inference that if p-zombies are conceivable, then they are metaphysically possible, which challenges physicalism.
The aspect of consciousness that consists of subjective experience or what it is like to be in a mental state.
The aspect of consciousness that makes mental content globally available for reasoning, speech, and action.
Awareness of oneself as an individual, including the ability to reflect on one's own mental states.
The phenomenon that conscious experience is typically integrated into a single, coherent whole rather than fragmented.
The belief that humans possess innate faculties for perceiving common truths, providing the foundation for knowledge.
The rejection of philosophical skepticism in favor of trusting common sense beliefs as inherently reliable.
Scottish philosopher associated with the Scottish Enlightenment and a proponent of common sense realism.
Scottish philosopher and poet who defended common sense philosophy against David Hume.
Scottish philosopher who further developed and taught common sense philosophy.
The view that concepts are represented by typical features and prototypes rather than strict definitions, challenging classical conceptual analysis.
The view that concepts are embedded in domain-specific naive theories, so that conceptual analysis involves investigating those theories.
The philosophical method of analyzing concepts through armchair reflection and thought experiments to uncover their content and structure.
nodes.id: Conceptual Analysis as A Priori Method

nodes.description: The classical tripartite analysis that knowledge is justified true belief, often attributed to Plato.
Knowledge that is justified independently of sensory experience, typically through reason or innate ideas.
Knowledge that is justified through sensory experience and empirical evidence.
The property of a belief that makes it epistemically permissible to hold, often a necessary condition for knowledge.
The thesis that the world has a beginning in time and is limited in space, and the antithesis that the world has no beginning and is infinite in space, both allegedly provable by pure reason.
The thesis that every composite substance consists of simple parts, and the antithesis that no composite thing consists of simple parts, leading to a contradiction about indivisible atoms.

The thesis that causality according to laws of nature is not sufficient to explain all phenomena, requiring a causality through freedom (spontaneity), and the antithesis that everything occurs solely according to natural laws, denying freedom.
nodes.id: Third antinomy (of spontaneity and causal determinism)

nodes.description: The thesis that there exists an absolutely necessary being belonging to the world as its cause, and the antithesis that no necessary being exists either in or outside the world, a contradiction about grounding existence.
The situation where two or more epistemic peers who share the same evidence hold conflicting beliefs, raising questions about rational belief revision.
The view that awareness of peer disagreement should lead one to significantly reduce confidence in one's belief, often by splitting the difference or suspending judgment.
The view that awareness of peer disagreement does not require one to reduce confidence, as one can rationally maintain one's original belief while acknowledging the disagreement.
A person who is equally likely to be correct as another, based on shared evidence, cognitive ability, and intellectual virtues.
A theory that truth is constructed through social processes and perspectives.
A theory that truth is what would be agreed upon by all rational inquirers under ideal conditions.
A theory that meaning of a word or sentence is determined by its use in language, as emphasized by Wittgenstein.
A theory that meaning is the mental idea or representation associated with a sign.
The medieval position that universals exist only as instantiated in particular objects, not as separate entities.
The view that aesthetic properties such as beauty are objective features of objects, existing independently of any perceiver.
A neologism combining 'difference' and 'deferral' to indicate that meaning never fully exists in a stable present but is constituted through a endless play of differences and delays.
The idea that every sign or meaning bears a trace of the absent other, a ghostly presence that unsettles claims to self-identity and pure presence.
The Western philosophical assumption of a fixed, foundational center of meaning (e.g., truth, God, reason) that privileges presence and speech over writing and absence.
The hierarchical dualities (e.g., speech/writing, presence/absence, nature/culture) that structure Western thought; deconstruction aims to reverse and displace these hierarchies.
A concept that shows how what is deemed secondary or supplementary (e.g., writing) is actually essential to what it supposedly adds to, undermining the original's self-sufficiency.
A subtype of Entity that includes material objects and processes, often the focus of scientific and empirical investigation.
A subtype of Entity that includes non-physical entities such as sets, relations, quantities, and propositions.
A subtype of Physical that refers to persistent, self-connected entities with spatial extent, such as physical objects.
A subtype of Physical that refers to changes over time, such as events or processes.
A subtype of Abstract that represents collections of entities defined by membership criteria.
A subtype of Abstract that represents properties or qualities that entities can have.
An entity that persists through time while maintaining its identity and can change.
An entity that unfolds over time, having temporal parts and occurring as a process or event.
A continuant that exists independently of other entities, such as a material object or organism.
A continuant that depends on an independent continuant for its existence, including qualities and realizable entities.
A specifically dependent continuant that can be realized in processes, such as roles, functions, or dispositions.
The study of reasoning using formal languages and mathematical methods to analyze deductive validity.
The study of reasoning in natural language, focusing on argumentation, fallacies, and critical thinking.
A deductive argument form with two premises and a conclusion, central to Aristotelian logic.
A system of logic that deals with logical operators (and, or, not, implication) on propositions.
An extension of propositional logic that includes quantifiers (all, some) and predicates to represent relations and properties.
A set of symbols and formation rules that define the well-formed formulas of a logical system.
The collection of axioms and inference rules used to derive theorems within a logical system.
The interpretation of the formal language, assigning meaning to its symbols and truth conditions to its formulas.
An operator in logic that expresses the extent to which a statement is true over a domain, such as 'for all' or 'there exists'.
A symbol or word used to connect propositions in logic, such as 'and', 'or', 'not', 'implies', which determine the truth value of the compound proposition.
A symbol that stands for an unspecified individual from the domain, used in forming quantified statements.
The set of entities over which quantifiers range and variables take values.
A mathematical structure consisting of a domain and an interpretation of the language, used to define truth in first-order logic.
The individual self or soul, considered the innermost essence of a being, often identified with Brahman in non-dualistic philosophies.
The concept of Brahman without attributes or qualities, beyond human comprehension and description.
The concept of Brahman with attributes, often personified as a personal deity, making the absolute accessible for worship.
The philosophical claim that the individual self (Atman) is identical to the ultimate reality (Brahman), central to Advaita Vedanta.
The principle of moral causation in Indian traditions where every action generates future consequences that determine the quality and nature of rebirth within saṃsāra.
The process by which a being's consciousness or soul continues after death into a new life form, driven by karma, as part of the endless cycle of saṃsāra.
The ultimate liberation or release from the cycle of saṃsāra, achieved through spiritual realization, ethical living, or other means depending on the tradition.
The material or substance out of which something is composed.
The form, pattern, or essence that defines what something is.
The agent or source that brings about change or motion.
The purpose, end, or goal for which something exists or is done.
The core idea that all phenomena lack intrinsic, independent essence (svabhava) and are empty of self-nature.
The distinction between conventional reality (samvriti-satya) and ultimate reality (paramartha-satya), where emptiness is the ultimate truth.
The principle that all dharmas arise in dependence on causes and conditions, which grounds and explains the emptiness of all phenomena.
In Indian philosophy, the term for orthodox schools that accept the authority of the Vedas as a valid means of knowledge.
In the Rigveda, Purusha is the primordial cosmic being whose sacrifice creates the universe, representing the universal spirit from which all things emanate.
In Samkhya philosophy, Purusha is the eternal, unchanging, non-material pure consciousness that is distinct from Prakriti (matter), with multiple individual purushas.
In Advaita Vedanta, Purusha is identical to Brahman, the ultimate non-dual reality, and is the true self of all beings.
The fundamental, uncaused, unmanifest source of the material world in Samkhya philosophy, which is the basis for all evolution.
Three innate qualities of Prakriti whose dynamic equilibrium and imbalance produce all phenomena in reality.
The metaphysical dualism between Prakriti (active, material principle) and Purusha (passive, eternal consciousness) as the foundation of reality.
The process of evolution of 23 principles (tattvas) from Prakriti, including intellect, ego, mind, and the five elements, forming the universe.
The concept in Advaita Vedanta that the phenomenal world is an illusion (maya) superimposed on the single reality of Brahman, caused by the creative power of Brahman itself.
In Buddhist philosophy, maya is one of the twenty subsidiary unwholesome mental factors, representing deceit or concealment regarding the illusory nature of phenomena.
In early Vedic texts, maya denotes a supernatural or magical power, often associated with gods like Indra and Varuna, used to create deceptive appearances or marvels.
The doctrine that all monads are synchronized by God such that their perceptions and actions are coordinated without direct causal interaction.
The principle that for every fact or event there is a reason why it is so and not otherwise, with God as the ultimate reason.
The monad as the first principle and supreme being in Pythagorean philosophy, from which all numbers and existence derive.
An indivisible, immaterial simple substance that is the fundamental unit of reality in Leibniz's monadology, each monad unique and reflecting the entire universe without interacting causally.
The all-inclusive whole that is the ultimate reality, in which all finite things are subsumed.
The doctrine that thinking subject and known object are fundamentally identical, enabling knowledge of the world.
A method of reasoning that proceeds through contradiction and resolution via thesis, antithesis, and synthesis.
The self-conscious, dynamic mind or absolute subject that manifests through history and culture.
The process of preserving, negating, and uplifting contradictory elements into a higher unity.
The concrete ethical order realized in social institutions like family, civil society, and the state.
The doctrine that will is the fundamental principle of reality, asserting that the ultimate nature of the universe is will or will-like, often associated with Schopenhauer.
The view that will is the primary factor in human psychology, emphasizing the role of will over intellect in action and decision-making.
The doctrine that God's will is the basis of morality and reality, often contrasting with intellectualist views that emphasize God's reason.
The position that moral obligations are grounded in the will, such as divine command theory or the idea that moral value arises from acts of willing.
The socialist doctrine that human will and effort can overcome material constraints to advance development, even without advanced technology or resources.
The ontological thesis that the world consists of independent, simple atomic facts that combine to form complex facts.
The theory that propositions are pictures of reality, sharing a logical form with the facts they depict.
The semantic doctrine that the meaning of a proposition is given by its method of verification.
The method of decomposing complex propositions into their simplest logical constituents to reveal the structure of thought and reality.
A type of necessity grounded solely in the laws of logic; a proposition is logically necessary if its negation is a logical contradiction.
A type of necessity grounded in the actual laws of physics; a proposition is physically necessary if it follows from the laws of nature.
The notion, associated with Kripke, that some necessary truths (e.g., 'Water is H2O') are knowable only through empirical experience, not a priori reasoning.
The distinction between beings (ontic) and Being as such (ontological), central to Heidegger's fundamental ontology.
The being (human existence) for whom the meaning of Being is an issue and who understands Being.
Heidegger's inquiry into the meaning of Being, which he argues has been forgotten by the Western metaphysical tradition.
The accusation that Western philosophy mistakenly treats Being as an ultimate entity (ontic) rather than the meaning of being.
The view that there are objective answers to the basic questions of ontology about what exists.
The view that ontology aims to determine what exists by analyzing the ontological commitments of theories.
The view that ontology aims to determine which entities are fundamental and how non-fundamental entities depend on them.
The view that there are no objective answers to ontological questions, typically holding that existence claims are relative to a linguistic framework.

The distinction between appearances (phenomena), which are objects of experience as shaped by our cognitive faculties, and things-in-themselves (noumena), which exist independently of our perception and cannot be known directly.
Space and time are not objective features of the world but are the a priori forms of our sensible intuition, structuring all sensory experience.
The a priori concepts (e.g., causality, substance, unity) that the understanding applies to intuitions to constitute objective experience and knowledge.
Judgments that are both necessary and universal (a priori) yet extend our knowledge (synthetic), such as those in mathematics and pure natural science, which Kant seeks to justify.
The self-conscious 'I think' that must accompany all representations, unifying them into a single experience and making possible the application of the categories.
A logic that extends first-order logic by allowing quantification over predicates and relations, enabling expression of properties about properties.
A formal system for defining computable functions via abstraction and application, used to model higher-order functions and variable binding.
The concept of quantifying over all predicates, functions, or sets of any order, central to higher-order logic's expressive power.
The distinction where abstract objects encode properties while concrete objects exemplify them.
For any set of properties, there exists exactly one abstract object that encodes that set.
The view that mathematical statements do not describe an objective mathematical reality but are justified by proof or constructive methods.
The position that the truth of a statement is determined by its assertability conditions rather than by correspondence to an external reality.
The view that scientific theories do not aim to describe an unobservable reality but are merely instruments for prediction or empirically adequate.
The denial that moral statements refer to objective moral facts or that moral properties exist independently of minds.
The view that knowledge does not involve a direct grasp of mind-independent reality, often emphasizing our conceptual schemes.
The view that mental and physical events occur in parallel without causal interaction, coordinated by a pre-established harmony.
The view that objects have a substance or inner nature that defines what they are, with essential properties that are necessary for identity.
The modern theory, advanced by Kripke and Putnam, that objects have necessary properties in a modal sense, such as essential origins or natural kind membership.
The concept of freedom from external interference or coercion, often associated with Isaiah Berlin.
The concept of freedom to achieve one's potential and self-mastery, often associated with Isaiah Berlin.
A legal and political concept balancing individual freedom with societal order under the rule of law.
The metaphysical view that matter is the sole fundamental substance and all entities, including mind and consciousness, are dependent on material interactions.
The thesis that mental states and processes are identical to or entirely reducible to physical processes in the brain.
The view that the meaning of life is determined by an individual's subjective preferences, goals, or sense of fulfillment.
The idea that life's meaning is derived from fulfilling a specific purpose or end, often linked to function or design.
The view that human life has no objective meaning, purpose, or intrinsic value.
The view that knowledge is impossible or that no truth can be known.
The view that the world or reality itself does not exist, or that nothingness is fundamental.
The rejection of all political authority, social institutions, and traditional values as groundless.
The view that each particular mental event or token is identical to some physical event, but does not require type-level identities.
The view that mental properties or phenomena can be fully reduced to physical properties via logical or nomological reduction.
The view that mental properties supervene on physical properties but are not reducible to them, often appealing to multiple realizability.
The thesis that the mental supervenes on the physical, meaning no mental change without a physical change, often used to formulate physicalism.
The view that moral judgments, such as right and wrong, are relative to the cultural or individual perspective and not universally objective.
The view that knowledge, justification, and rationality are relative to the conceptual, cultural, or historical framework and cannot be objectively assessed.
The view that what exists, or the nature of reality, is relative to a conceptual scheme or language and admits of no absolute characterization.
The view that judgments of beauty and artistic value are relative to cultural or personal standards and lack universal validity.
The view that the validity of logical principles and rules of inference varies across different conceptual frameworks or cultures.
The theory that reality is fundamentally dependent on perception or consciousness, and that there is no objective reality independent of mental activity.
The metaethical view that moral judgments are based on individual feelings, attitudes, or preferences rather than objective moral facts.
The interpretation of probability as a degree of belief or subjective certainty, rather than objective frequency or propensity.
A foundational formal system that assigns types to entities, uses functions as primitive, and avoids set-theoretic paradoxes through a hierarchy of types.
A type theory introduced by Russell that introduces orders (levels) to types to prevent impredicative definitions and resolve logical paradoxes.
The philosophical identification of propositions with types of their proofs, formalized by the Curry-Howard correspondence between logic and computation.
A distinction between type theories where identity types may have distinct proofs (intensional) or are collapsed (extensional), affecting notions of equality and identity.
The thesis that cognitive processes are fundamentally shaped by the body's sensory-motor systems and its interactions with the environment.
The claim that cognitive processes can extend beyond the brain and body to include environmental resources such as tools and notebooks.
The view that cognition is deeply dependent on the context and physical situation in which it occurs, not just internal representations.
A measure of information based solely on the statistical properties of signals, as defined by Shannon's information theory.
Information that carries meaning, truth conditions, or propositional content, central to philosophy of language and epistemology.
Information defined by its utility or effect on a receiver's beliefs or actions, often studied in decision theory and philosophy of mind.
In philosophy, datum is a single piece of information or observation, considered as the basic unit of empirical experience.
The concept that data serves as support for scientific theories or everyday beliefs, central to confirmation theory.
The epistemological notion that sensory data are directly presented to the mind, a concept criticized in analytic philosophy.
The idea that intelligence is a single, unified mental capacity underlying all cognitive abilities, often measured by IQ tests.
The theory that intelligence consists of several distinct modalities (e.g., linguistic, logical-mathematical, spatial) rather than a single general factor.
The distinction between the innate ability to solve novel problems (fluid) and knowledge acquired through experience (crystallized).
A behavioral test for machine intelligence proposed by Alan Turing, focusing on the ability to exhibit human-like conversational behavior.
The view that the human mind is a kind of computational system, and that intelligent cognition is a form of information processing.
The study of the nature, sources, and limits of intelligent knowledge and understanding, including the concept of sapience and self-awareness.
A mental state of intending to perform a future action, involving a commitment to act and serving as a basis for planning.
The specific reason or purpose for which an action is performed, explaining why the agent acted and making the action intentional.
Action performed with a purpose or intention, distinguished from accidental or involuntary behavior by its directedness toward a goal.
The philosophical view that the mind accesses external reality through internal mental representations, which act as intermediaries between the observer and the world.
The theory in philosophy of mind that mental states (e.g., beliefs, desires) are characterized by their representational content, meaning they represent states of affairs in the world.
The modern scientific and philosophical concept of nature as a system governed by universal, discoverable regularities.
The Indian philosophical concept of primordial material nature as the fundamental substrate of all change and diversity.
The Chinese philosophical concept of the natural way or path that underlies and harmonizes the cosmos and human existence.
A 19th-century German Romantic philosophical school viewing nature as a dynamic, organic, and self-organizing whole.
The property of events or elements occurring in a predictable, repeated manner; a foundational concept in metaphysics and philosophy of science.
The recurrence of identical or similar elements over space or time; a key component of pattern formation and a concept in metaphysics and aesthetics.
The cognitive process of identifying regularities or structures; central to epistemology and philosophy of mind.
Patterns that are objectively real under some description, as discussed in philosophy of science and metaphysics.
The epistemological doubt about whether perception can provide knowledge of an external world, often rooted in the possibility of systematic deception.
The subjective, qualitative 'what it's like' aspect of conscious experience.
The apparent difficulty in explaining how physical processes give rise to subjective experiences.
Mary the color scientist knows all physical facts about color but learns something new upon seeing red, suggesting qualia are non-physical.
A thought experiment where two individuals have swapped color experiences but identical behavior, challenging functionalist accounts of qualia.
The debate over whether mental states are exhaustively defined by their causal roles, which seems to leave out qualia.
The view that the phenomenal character of experience is identical to its representational content.
The view that our introspective judgments about qualia are mistaken; qualia do not actually exist.
The self is not a static substance but a dynamic process of actualizing potential, as in Aristotle's conception of the psyche.
The self (soul or intellect) can exist apart from sensory experience and the body, as in Platonic and Cartesian dualism.
The self is a useful fiction, a narrative unity constructed by the brain to organize experience, as proposed by Daniel Dennett.
nodes.id: Self as a narrative center of gravity

nodes.description: The concept of self is merely a linguistic or syntactic construct without ontological weight.
The self is a point of view or subjective perspective through which experience is organized.
The self is an illusion; there is no permanent, unchanging self, and clinging to the idea of self causes suffering.
The idea that the soul is eternal and survives the death of the body, often with an afterlife or reincarnation.
Plato's division of the soul into rational, spirited, and appetitive parts, each responsible for different functions and virtues.
Aristotle's conception of the soul as the first actuality or form of a natural body with potential for life, explaining all vital and psychological functions.
nodes.id: Soul as form of the body

nodes.description: Epicurus' theory that the soul is composed of fine atoms dispersed throughout the body and is mortal, ceasing to exist at death.
The Stoic view that the soul is a portion of the divine material pneuma, a rational and fiery substance that pervades the body and enables perception and reason.
The Platonic doctrine that the soul exists before birth and possesses innate knowledge from its prior existence.
The belief that the soul passes from one body to another after death, often in a cycle of rebirth.
School of thought holding that true reality lies in eternal, immaterial Forms and that thinking is a process of recollecting these Forms.
The thesis that thinking consists in internalized silent speech, often tied to social development.
Theory that mental states and processes are explained by the formation and strengthening of associations between ideas.
View that thinking is a computational process, manipulating symbols according to rules.
A fundamental distinction in ethics between value that is inherent in an object (intrinsic) and value that derives from its relations or consequences (extrinsic).
The distinction between value that is universal and unchanging (absolute) and value that depends on context, perspective, or culture (relative).
An ordered set of ethical principles or priorities that guide decision-making and behavior.
A value that is regarded as absolute and not subject to trade-off or compromise in ethical deliberation.
A copy that either has no original or whose original has been lost, representing a representation that replaces reality.
The imitation of real-world processes or systems, leading to a condition where signs and symbols replace and supersede reality.
The process by which simulacra (copies without originals) precede and determine reality, making signs more fundamental than the real.
A condition in which simulation becomes indistinguishable from reality, creating a reality by models and signs with no referent.
A progression from signs reflecting reality, to perverting reality, to masking the absence of reality, to signs that bear no relation to reality (pure simulacrum).
The idea that natural entities possess inherent purposes or goals independent of human use or opinion.
The idea that purposes are imposed by an external agent or by human use, not inherent in the object itself.
An explanation that accounts for phenomena by citing their ends, purposes, or functions rather than efficient causes.
An aesthetic emotion associated with pleasure, harmony, and proportion, often linked to classical conceptions of beauty.
An aesthetic emotion involving awe, terror, and a sense of vastness or power, often evoked by nature or grand art.
An aesthetic emotion associated with sentimental, exaggerated, or low-quality art or objects that aim to provoke an immediate emotional response.
A form of consequentialism that holds that individuals should act in their own self-interest as the sole criterion of morality.
The view that the moral rightness of an action is determined solely by the actual consequences of that specific action.
The view that an action is right if it conforms to a set of rules whose general acceptance would produce the best overall consequences.
Kant's fundamental principle of morality that commands unconditionally, binding all rational agents regardless of desires.
The core idea that actions are morally right if they conform to a set of duties or rules, independent of consequences.
Ross's list of conditional duties (fidelity, reparation, gratitude, etc.) that are binding unless overridden by a stronger duty.
The deontological restriction that certain actions (e.g., killing, lying) are wrong even if doing them would produce better overall consequences.
The everyday conceptual framework of mental states such as beliefs, desires, and sensations used to explain and predict human behavior.
A concept from philosophy of science describing theory replacement where the new theory eliminates the ontology of the old, rather than reducing to it.
The idea that certain concepts, like phlogiston, may fail to refer to anything real and are dissolved in the process of scientific progress.

The principle that a single mental state type (e.g., pain) can be realized by different physical systems (biological, silicon, etc.), implying that mental states are not identical to specific brain states.

The view that mental states are defined entirely by their causal relations to sensory inputs, behavioral outputs, and other mental states, capturing their functional role in a system's mental economy.
A version of functionalism that models mental states as states of a Turing machine, where each state is defined by its place in a machine table specifying transitions from inputs and to outputs.
The approach that functional definitions of mental states are derived from the conceptual analysis of ordinary language and common-sense platitudes about mental concepts.
The meta-ethical view that all moral claims are false, leading to the conclusion that moral knowledge is impossible.
The meta-ethical view that we are never justified in believing any moral claim, while being agnostic about whether moral claims are false.
The meta-ethical view that moral claims lack truth-value, being expressions of emotion or imperatives, so moral knowledge is impossible.
A meta-ethical theory that defines moral judgments as those that would be approved by a hypothetical impartial, rational observer.
The meta-ethical position that moral obligations are derived from the commands of God or a divine being.
A non-cognitivist meta-ethical theory that moral statements are universal prescriptions that apply to all similar situations.
The philosophical position that there are multiple, incommensurable but universally applicable values that can conflict.
Events are structured particulars composed of an object, a property, and a time, defined by an object exemplifying a property at a time.
Events are primitive, unanalyzable particulars that can be described under multiple descriptions and function as basic ontological units.
Events are classes of possible spatiotemporal regions, analyzed in terms of counterpart theory and modal realism.
Events are virtual singularities distinct from actual states of affairs, embodying pure changes and multiplicities.
Events are radical ruptures in a given situation that are undecidable within it, revealing new truths and transforming ontology.
Events are foundational units of process philosophy, emphasizing becoming and dynamic change over static being.
The Jain theory of partial viewpoints, asserting that knowledge is always from a particular perspective and never absolute.
A Madhyamaka stance that uses autonomous syllogistic reasoning (svātantra) to argue for emptiness, accepting that things exist conventionally according to characteristics.
A Madhyamaka stance that relies solely on reductio ad absurdum (prasaṅga) to refute inherent existence, rejecting autonomous syllogisms and denying any conventional intrinsic nature.
The early Buddhist concept that the self (ātman) is empty, meaning there is no permanent, unchanging essence.
The Mādhyamaka school's view that all phenomena (dharmas) are empty of intrinsic nature (svabhāva).
The Yogācāra school's concept that external objects are empty, with only mind (citta) and mental phenomena existing.
The doctrine that all sentient beings have the potential for Buddhahood, often equated with emptiness in Mahayana.
The philosophical concept asserting a real and fundamental distinction between entities, such as individual souls and the Supreme Being, within the context of Vedantic dualism.
The philosophical concept affirming an underlying unity or identity between entities, positing that they are essentially one in nature, as in the qualitative oneness of soul and Brahman.
The epistemic concept that certain truths, particularly the simultaneous unity and difference of reality, transcend human reason and cannot be fully grasped by logic or analysis.
A metaphysical concept in Indian philosophy describing the real transformation of a cause (e.g., divine energy) into effect (e.g., material world), as opposed to mere appearance.
The metaphysical concept of a single, infinite, unchanging, and all-encompassing reality that underlies all existence.
The doctrine that the physical world is a real transformation of Brahman and not an illusion, opposing the Mayavada view of Advaita.
The concept that individual souls are not identical to Brahman but are inseparable parts of the divine whole, like sparks from a fire.
The concept of divine grace as the sole means to liberation, emphasizing devotion and the free will of God over ascetic effort.
Founder and chief philosopher of the Shuddhadvaita school, who articulated its doctrines in the 16th century.
Ancient Greek historian and philosopher whose account of the Peloponnesian War is considered a foundational text for classical realism, emphasizing power politics and human nature.
Renaissance political philosopher who argued that rulers should prioritize power and self-interest over morality, influencing classical realism's view of politics.
English philosopher who described the state of nature as a war of all against all, providing a basis for classical realism's pessimistic view of human nature and the need for a sovereign.

20th-century political scientist who systematized classical realism with his 'Six Principles of Political Realism', emphasizing objective laws of politics and national interest defined in terms of power.
The classical realist belief that humans are inherently self-interested, power-seeking, and prone to conflict, which is reflected in the behavior of states.
A theory in classical realism that states seek to prevent any one state from gaining dominance, thereby maintaining stability through counterbalancing alliances and military strength.
The philosophical view that the group or society is more important than the individual, and that individual interests should be subordinate to collective goals.
The epistemological principle that social and cultural phenomena can be fully understood only through their historical development and the contingent processes that shaped them.
The view that history unfolds through a dialectical process of thesis, antithesis, and synthesis, leading to the progressive realization of reason and freedom.
The doctrine, criticized by Karl Popper, that history is governed by inexorable laws that allow prediction of the future course of human affairs, leading to totalitarianism.
The approach in anthropology and sociology that explains cultural practices and beliefs through their historical origins and transformations.
The view that the meaning of a word or sentence depends on its relations to other words or sentences within the entire language system.
The view that a hypothesis cannot be confirmed or disconfirmed in isolation; only entire systems of beliefs face empirical test.
The view that wholes are more than the sum of their parts, and that the identity of parts is determined by their place in the whole.
The approach that explains social or natural phenomena by studying the whole system rather than its individual components in isolation.
The idea that concepts like 'human nature', 'man', and 'humanity' are historically relative, ideological, or metaphysical constructs rather than universal essences.
Nietzsche argued that humanism is a secular form of theism and that human rights serve to empower the weak, denying life.
Marx viewed human rights as symptoms of dehumanization under capitalism, necessary because individuals are alienated and in conflict.
nodes.id: Karl Marx's critique of human rights

nodes.description: The structuralist approach (influenced by Saussure, Lévi-Strauss, and Althusser) that denies a constitutive human subject, treating systems and structures as primary.
The post-structuralist critique (Foucault, Derrida, Deleuze) that deconstructs the human subject as a product of discourse, power, and difference.
The view that scientific knowledge and method supersede the humanities, reducing human meaning to natural phenomena.
nodes.id: Positivism and scientism as antihumanist traditions

nodes.description: The metaphysical doctrine that non-material, abstract ideas (Forms) are the most fundamental reality, giving shape to the physical world.
The view that nature and mind are essentially identical, distinguished only by perspective, and that reality is constituted by rational spiritual elements.
The theory that matter is effete mind, with physical laws being ingrained habits of mind, and that material objects evolve from feelings.
The principle that social phenomena should be explained solely by the actions and properties of individual agents.
A political philosophy that emphasizes individual rights, liberty, and equality.
The capacity of an individual to make reasoned, self-governing decisions.
The idea that there exists a set of moral truths inherent in nature that can be known through rational reflection, independent of human convention.
nodes.id: Universal moral principles discoverable by reason

nodes.description: The jurisprudential principle that human-made laws are valid only if they align with objective moral standards derived from nature or reason.
The Aristotelian-Thomistic version of natural law that grounds moral and legal norms in the inherent purposes (telos) of natural beings and human nature.
The Stoic doctrine that natural law is the rational principle (logos) that governs the cosmos and that humans should live in accordance with this universal reason.
The idea that individuals possess inherent rights (such as life, liberty, property) that are derived from natural law and precede any political authority.
The metaphysical view that all entities and phenomena can be reduced to a single fundamental substance or level of reality.
The epistemological view that knowledge of a complex system is wholly derivable from knowledge of its simpler components.
The methodological approach that explains complex systems exclusively by analyzing their constituent parts and their interactions.
The philosophical model of intertheoretic reduction where one scientific theory is logically deduced from a more fundamental theory via bridge laws.
The view that the meanings of complex expressions can be analyzed in terms of simpler, directly meaningful terms.
A hypothetical scenario describing life without government, where individuals are free and equal, and which provides the motivation for establishing a social contract.
The idea that legitimate political authority depends on the voluntary agreement (explicit or tacit) of those governed.
The moral duty of individuals to obey the laws and authority of a state that results from a legitimate social contract.
The normative property that a government or set of social rules possesses when they can be justified by the agreement of those subject to them.
The foundational normative principle that actions are right if they promote the greatest good for the greatest number.
The method proposed by Jeremy Bentham for measuring pleasure and pain quantitatively to determine the moral value of an action.
The version of utilitarianism that evaluates each individual action directly by its consequences.
The version of utilitarianism that evaluates actions based on whether they conform to rules that, if generally followed, produce the best consequences.
A form of utilitarianism that focuses on satisfying preferences rather than maximizing pleasure.
An English philosopher and social reformer who founded classical utilitarianism and developed the hedonic calculus.
A British philosopher and economist who refined utilitarianism, emphasizing qualitative differences in pleasures.
The claim that the existence and content of law depend on social facts such as acts of legislation, court decisions, and customs, not on moral considerations.
The thesis that law and morality are conceptually distinct; there is no necessary connection between what law is and what it ought to be.
The principle that the validity of a legal norm is determined by its social sources (e.g., enactment by a recognized authority) rather than its moral content.
The view that moral discourse attempts to state objective moral facts but systematically fails because no such facts exist.
The view that basic moral truths are self-evident and known through intellectual intuition.
The view that moral principles are known through reason alone, independent of empirical observation.
The view that moral statements are truth-apt, i.e., they can be true or false, as opposed to non-cognitivist theories like expressivism.
The view that moral truths are mind-independent and objective, not relative to individuals or cultures.
The ontological claim that there are moral facts or properties in reality, i.e., that at least some moral propositions are true.
The doctrine that scientific knowledge is the only legitimate form of knowledge.
The claim that only scientific methods can yield reliable knowledge.
The metaphysical thesis that only entities studied by science are real.
The epistemological stance that observer, process of observing, and observed object are inseparable in any inquiry, rejecting dualisms.
A metaphysical view that rejects self-action and interaction, instead seeing reality as composed of transactions where entities are defined by their mutual relations.
The epistemological principle that truth is not a static correspondence but emerges through ongoing, fallible inquiry.
An ethical view that moral values arise from co-constitutive relationships and trans-dermal experiences across organism-environment boundaries.
A political philosophy viewing cooperation as a form of collective inquiry where individuals are co-constitutive with their social and natural environment.
An aesthetic theory where value and satisfaction emerge from the transactional relation between an organism and its assumptive world.
The principle that individual existence and flourishing are fundamentally tied to the community, often summarized as 'I am because we are'.
The ethical and social value that prioritizes group harmony, mutual support, and collective decision-making over individual autonomy.
The ethical demand to treat others with empathy, compassion, and respect as an extension of one's own humanity.
The doctrine that reality consists solely in the act of thinking, not in any static thoughts or objects.
The concept that the mind creates itself through its own activity, a form of self-positing.
The epistemological claim that in self-consciousness there is no distinction between the knower and the known.
The monistic view that all reality is contained within the thinking consciousness, denying any external independent matter.
A moral principle asserting a duty to both implement ideals of virtue or good and make necessary compromises to realize them, rejecting the extremes of pure idealism and pure expediency.
The central Yogacara doctrine that all phenomena are mere mental representations, with no external reality independent of consciousness.
The foundational consciousness that stores all karmic seeds and serves as the basis for the arising of all cognitive and perceptual experiences.
The Yogacara theory that all phenomena can be analyzed into three aspects: the imagined (parikalpita), the dependent (paratantra), and the perfected (parinishpanna) natures.
The Yogacara model of eight types of consciousness, including the five sense consciousnesses, the mental consciousness, the ego-consciousness (manas), and the store consciousness (ālaya-vijñāna).
A key founder of Yogacara philosophy, credited with composing foundational texts and systematizing the doctrine of consciousness-only.
The view that the mental properties of composite entities are constituted by the mental properties of their fundamental parts.
The view that macro-level consciousness emerges from but is not simply the sum of micro-level mental features.
The view that fundamental entities possess protomental properties that are precursors to full mentality.
The view that mentality is fundamental at the micro-level, existing in the smallest constituents of reality.
The view that the universe as a whole is a conscious entity and that cosmic consciousness is fundamental.
The philosophical difficulty of explaining how micro-level subjective experiences combine to form a unified macro-level consciousness.
Knowledge about one's own cognitive processes, including beliefs about strategies, abilities, and task demands.
The active monitoring and control of one's own cognitive processes, such as planning, checking, and evaluating.
Affective or cognitive experiences that accompany cognitive tasks, such as feelings of knowing, confidence, or difficulty.
A design philosophy that imitates nature's ecosystemic interdependencies to achieve sustainable and synergistic outcomes.
A collaborative design approach where all stakeholders actively participate in shaping outcomes.
A holistic way of understanding complex systems by focusing on interconnections and patterns rather than isolated parts.
The property by which complex systems produce novel, unpredicted behaviors from the interaction of simpler components.
A hermeneutic method in Metapsychiatry that asks 'What is the meaning of what seems to be?' and 'What is what really is?' to reorient from material appearances to spiritual reality.
Acronym for Peace, Assurance, Gratitude, Love, considered in Metapsychiatry as qualities indicating a state of spiritual consciousness.
The Metapsychiatric conception of God as a limitless, non-material, loving intelligence, also called Love-Intelligence.
Metapsychiatry distinguishes between religious practice and spiritual interest, emphasizing direct spiritual understanding over formal religion.
American psychiatrist (1914–1995) who developed Metapsychiatry as a spiritual form of psychotherapy based on metaphysical concepts.
The concept that a large part of mental life consists of thoughts, memories, and desires that are not accessible to conscious awareness but influence behavior.
The principle that all mental events, including seemingly random thoughts and feelings, have a causal explanation based on prior psychological states.
The division of the psyche into three interacting agencies: the id (instinctual drives), the ego (reality-oriented conscious self), and the superego (moral and idealistic conscience).
The philosophical position that there are objective, mind-independent epistemic facts about knowledge, justification, and related notions.
The denial of the objectivity of epistemic facts, either by rejecting their existence or asserting their mind-dependence.
